Instructor Details

Abdullah Muzahid

Webpage:  https://people.tamu.edu/~abdullah.muzahid/index.html

Biography

I am an Associate Professor in the department of Computer Science

and Engineering at Texas A&M University. I have joined here in Fall

2028. My research area is broadly computer architecture and systems

with recent focus on machine learning and security.

Catalog Description

Microcomputer Systems. (2-2). Credit 3. Microcomputers as components of

systems; VLSI processor and coprocessor architectures, addressing and instruction

sets; I/O interfaces and supervisory control; VLSI architectures for signal processing;

integrating special purpose processors into a system. Prerequisite: CSCE 313.

Additional Course Details

This class aims to guide students to make use of their knowledge from prior classes to

develop the best practice in design and implement of novel ideas into tangible prototype

systems. The class will guide students through some  basic procedures and logistics in

system development and prototyping to tailor their own developments to achieve the project

goals. Every application has its own requirements and resource constraints. The class will

develop the best practice in design and implement of novel ideas into tangible prototype

systems. The class will guide students through some  basic procedures and logistics in

system development and prototyping to tailor their own developments to achieve the project

goals. Every application has its own requirements and resource constraints. The class will

guide students through the baseline development process to achieve their own unique

project goals.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 313.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Initiate, formulate, plan an open project;

Analyze and choose microcontroller, software technologies, and physical

fixtures to meet functional requirements;

Integrate the hardware, software modules, and the physical fixture to

manifest the application scenarios:

Make a live demonstration of the project prototype.

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Required

No Textbook Needed

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Schedule

To achieve the leaning goals,  the class is organized into three main pillars of

the  activities in two phases.

Phase I: Lecture and tests, and lab exercises. (5-6 weeks)

Phase II: Term projects. (remaining weeks)

Lectures

Using successfully implemented class projects in the past as the

references, the lectures are a series of tear down of these past prototypes

for students to guide students through major technical elements and

challenges that need to be overcome to transform an idea into a working

system.

The lectures focus on

(a) description of system objectives from system designers’ perspectives,

then

(b) formulation of the system design problems,

(c) key challenges in solving the problems, and

for students to guide students through major technical elements and

challenges that need to be overcome to transform an idea into a working

system.

The lectures focus on

(a) description of system objectives from system designers’ perspectives,

then

(b) formulation of the system design problems,

(c) key challenges in solving the problems, and

(d) the solutions and demonstration from designers’ perspectives.

One Test

Both the lectures and lab contents are used in the single test.

Guided Lab Exercises

Students are organized into  groups to carry out a number of hands-on lab

exercises.

Term Projects

The final project is based on the  development process of

(a) initiation of idea,

(b) development of a work plan,

(c) execution of the project development, and

(d) final review of the project.

The project development is phased into two checkpoints, each of which has

defined progress goals, followed by the final project review.

AI Statement

Unless otherwise specified, the use of Automated Writing Tools, including ChatGPT and

similar artificial intelligence (AI) tools, is strictly prohibited in this course, even when

properly attributed. The use of automated writing tools is considered plagiarism and will be

handled in accordance with existing policy.

Course Specific Attendance Policy

For excused absences, an opportunity will be provided to make up any

graded work that was missed. For unexcused absences, a grade of zero will

be assigned for a missed quiz or in-class assignment. Missed exams will be

rescheduled without penalty for an excused absence, or with a 25% penalty

if the absence is not excused, and at approval of the instructor.

To request approval of an absence, send me an e-mail explaining the reason

for the absence  prior to the class or lab . If advanced notification is not

possible (e.g., unexpected illness) send the e-mail within 48 hours of the

absence and be sure to explain why you were not able to notify me in

advance. For illness, follow-up the e-mail by submitting a note from a doctor

or clinic.
